summary: Formal cadastral survey from Huacho (Huaura Valley, central coast) using systematically DOUBLED vocabulary — every key term reduplicated (qaqa->qaqaqaqa x6, mama->mamamama x2, papa->papapapa, kaka->kakakaka) = emphatic/formal register style. Deep L4 tree header establishes surveyed area: wall-rock complex (kaqaqaqaqaqa) with bridge (chaka) and verification (chiqa). Data block totals 209. Lineage-based land registration: each rock named after holding lineage. Goteborg museum. 77 cords, 21 STRING, 52% coverage (transparent compounds lower rate).
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  77 cords (L1: ~60, L2: 7, L3: 3, L4: 1). 21 STRING (27%, 52% hits), 28 INT, 28 EMPTY (36%).
  Architecture: 1-PAIRE with deep L4 tree in header. 8 clusters.
  5 colors: AB(33), W(28), KB(6), GG(5), MB(3).
  Provenance: Huacho, Huaura Valley. Museum: Worldculture, Goteborg (1938.45.0229).
  OKR: Approximate knot positions on multiple cords.

DOUBLING PATTERN
  Geography: qaqa -> qaqaqaqa (x6), paqa -> paqaqaqa, chiqa -> chiqaqaqa, kaqa -> kaqaqaqaqaqa.
  Kinship: mama -> mamamama (x2), papa -> papapapa, kaka -> kakakaka.
  Systematic FORMAL REGISTER STYLE — doubling marks emphasis/plurality/official status.

HEADER TREE (L4 depth)
  C1 (INT=1) -> C3: kaqaqaqaqaqa (wall-rock-rock-rock) -> C5: chiqa chaqa (truth + bridge) -> C6: pakiqa (to break-TOPIC) -> C7: kikiqa (self-TOPIC). Top-down: 'Concerning the wall-rock complex: truth about the bridge. The breaking. The self.'

TEXT SECTION (cords 37-47)
  C37: kaqa chiqa takaqa (wall, truth, strike-TOPIC) = cadastral verification.
  C38: ki chaka (bridge). C39 (GG): mamamama (mother-mother). C40: chiqaqaqa (truth-rock).
  C42-47: qaqaqaqa x4 = four named rock landmarks. C43: kakakaka (uncle-uncle). C44: kamama-PASS (governance directed).

LINEAGE SECTION (cords 53-59)
  mamamama (mother), papapapa (father), qaqaqaqa (their rock/land), TOP-kikiqa (about the self on GG/justice color).

NARRATIVE — THE DOUBLED ROCKS OF HUACHO
  Rock, rock. That is what this khipu counts — the word *qaqaqaqa*, doubled, six times. Not just *qaqa* but *qaqa-qaqa*, the rock named twice as if to say: this is an OFFICIAL rock, a formal landmark, a surveyed and registered feature of the terrain.
  The document opens with a tree that descends four levels. At the top, a single value: 1. Then, nested inside: *kaqaqaqaqaqa* — the wall, the rock, the rock, the rock, stacked in Aymara and Quechua. The truth (*chiqa*) next to the bridge (*chaqa*). The breaking (*pakiqa*). And at the bottom, four levels down: *kikiqa* — the self.
  Nine cords of white data — 20, 38, 20, 16, 31, 27, 26, 16, 15 — totaling 209 units for the first zone.
  The text section names landmarks in formal doubled vocabulary. Four consecutive *qaqaqaqa* entries — four rocks, each named twice. Between them, the lineages: *mamamama* (cord 39) — the mother's line, doubled for formality, on the gray cord of justice. *Kakakaka* (cord 43) — the uncle's line. *Kamama-PASS* (cord 44) — the governance was directed.
  Then the full lineage register: mother-mother, father-father, and the rocks that belong to them. On the justice cord, the self named one final time: *TOP-kikiqa*. And then a single, un-doubled *qaqa* — just 'rock,' alone, informal, as if the scribe dropped the formality for one cord.
  The doubling is not a reading error. It is a style — a formal register, as distinct from informal speech as a legal brief is from a conversation. The scribe at Huacho wrote like a notary: every noun doubled, every landmark certified twice.
references: Sivan 2026, ALBA Project — Reduplicated forms analysis, formal register style
Huacho — Huaura Valley, central coast Inca administrative center
Cross-corpus: UR050 (Soldi formal text, paradigmatic forms), UR1095 (Pachacamac cadastral)
